speaker: interviewer
text: --April 1996. What stands out most vividly in your mind, from your time in the Krakow ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were a few incidents, which I have been aware of. There was a factory called Optima. And people were going there to work. And we all wanted to go there to work. And then one day, we have heard trucks have arrived, loaded a lot of people. From that Optima, they have pulled out a lot of people. And they were shot. And some of them-- like, daughter wanted to go on that truck with mother-- well, she was just shot like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see that?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. But that was so very impressed upon me. Because Optima was a factory before the war, which I knew. And we all tried to get work at Optima. It was a sitting job. It was sewing. And later on, that place, Optima, became a gathering point for Jews who were going on the trucks. And that was the end. That was extremely sad. And this is-- we knew whoever was taken to Optima, people who were-- soldiers, SS would come with a gun, collect few people, bring them to Optima, lock them in, went for a next lot, constantly. And you know, you're just sort of grateful at that moment it wasn't you. And the perfect way to avoid that was to hide, to get off the street, to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember another incident, which is haunting me. There was [NON-ENGLISH], like what is it called, that place here, where you go to get [NON-ENGLISH], work? Social security?

speaker: interviewee
text: Social security, but it wasn't called social-- something. [NON-ENGLISH], a place to get-- a bureau of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Employment agency?

speaker: interviewee
text: Employment agency, right. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we would want to go to get work. My sister went, and I went. And there was a woman called Sandberg and a German called [NON-ENGLISH]. And he was selecting whom he wanted to get on that truck to work. And that was really to work. You sensed-- you knew when it was to work, and when it was not. When you were surrounded by SS with the gun, you knew this is not going just to work. It's more than that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we all tried to get on that truck. And a lot of us went on. I certainly scrambled. And my sister came.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was a truck-- open truck, you know, with those things that you held onto. In the back, there were seats on each side. But there were about 50 people on that truck. Some of them managed to sit. And most of us were standing. And they were driving very fast. And at one point, taking a corner, lots of people got spilled out of that truck, including my sister. I was still on the truck holding on. And she had injuries. She injured her neck. She was bleeding. And we were very concerned that once you are injured and you are bleeding, they will not bring us back to the ghetto. They will shoot us. But somehow, we did come back to the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a terrifying experience. Can you imagine Jews lying on the road, cars going by, trams going by? But we helped each other.

speaker: interviewee
text: I jumped off the truck, got back onto the truck. But she had broken teeth. To this day, when we talk about that incident, we both shudder. But that was the time that I managed to go to work without the blue paper. [SIGHS] There were incidents which I remember in ghetto. Many times, SS men just came and didn't like somebody, just for amusement, in particular when they were drunk. Or they'd just shoot you. And that was that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. I did. I saw it. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, look. You see a person approaching. And there go the SS men, normal, not shouting, not being conspicuous in any way. They were walking into the ghetto for no reason, or to go to the office. And out of, out of a blue, they pulled out a gun. They shot somebody for no reason whatsoever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the best way was just to go on. You didn't stop to give help to that person who was lying dead. You just went on as if nothing happened, you didn't see anything. And you managed to get to the first available gate, to avoid the SS turning around and shooting you. Yes, I have seen that. And what was going through your mind, while that was-- while you were going through the first gate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not even terrified. Numbness. Total numbness. Total numbness. I came home and I told my mother and my father what has happened. They already knew about it. Ghetto was fairly small. And that just-- they impressed more upon me I shouldn't go out. I should stay inside the room. All we had in ghetto was a tiny little room-- window, bed, curtain-- where my parents slept. And we were at the other end. Stove for cooking, if there was anything to cook. And that was it. And I was a young girl. It was to be scooped up in that way for a long period, it was not very pleasant. So one would venture, in spite of the fact that it was dangerous. There was a dentist. I used to look for excuse to go to that dentist, [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a very good dentist and very pleasant-looking. So I always had sore tooth to go to him. That was across the street from us-- just, just to get out of that. Well, that lasted till about January or February, '42 or '43. '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents heard that the ghetto is going to be liquidated. And there is-- they're opening a camp in Plaszow. And that proved to be true.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother equipped me. I was given good coat and warm clothes. Because it was winter, height of winter. And I went with the very first transport to Plaszow. Where was Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Plaszow was very short distance from Krakow. I used to run around there. It was on the site of Jewish cemetery. Part of it was a cemetery. And part of it was just open area, absolutely nothing, just empty, beautiful green area. And there we were brought-- dark, cold. Always dark-- when I think of camp, it was always dark and always cold. And it couldn't have been. It must have-- sometime the sun must have shining. But my association with Plaszow-- it was cold always and, and no sun.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first went there, was the camp established?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. We were the ones who were supposed to build the camp. And that's where I have encountered Goeth, Amon Goeth. There were about 50 women and-- maybe 50 or a little bit more, a few more, men. And we were digging the foundation for the block, to which later the rest of the people came from ghetto. Well, so we stood there with a pick-axe and tried to dig in that hard earth, frozen. Because it was January, maybe end of January, beginning of February. I can't remember exactly. I remember the, the weather. And that tells me that it was freezing cold. As well as we were dressed, it was very cold.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we tried to dig. There was somebody who showed us how far and how deep. And that's where the block was supposed to be standing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on one occasion right at the beginning, Amon Goeth arrived, enormously tall.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was the commandant of the Plaszow camp. Looked enormous, looked very well-dressed, very well-groomed. Proportionally fat to his height. I'm sure that he was at least 7-foot tall. Excessively tall, excessively fat, but proportional. And he walked with a gait. You know, sort of, you can almost admire him, till he showed us what he can-- And on that first encounter, he must have been watching us with binoculars. And he saw that we were just huddling together, to get a bit of warmth and talking. To dig in that dreadful circumstances. We couldn't even get that pick-axe into the earth. And he came with a whip and started to whip us. Pulled one elderly m whom he said-- oh, when he was approaching, we immediately started to dig and work and disperse, like we were supposed to. But he picked up a woman and shot her. Just like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw this.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Not did I only see it. But I averted my head and looked. What are we going to do? We're going to help her? We're going to pick her up? Who is going to do something? I would say that I was the youngest of the group.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been 14. '43, 14. 14, maybe a little bit more, 14 and a half. In July, I would have been 14, 13. I don't know. Work it out with the, with the

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not pleased to tell you how old I am now. But then it was all right. So [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we knew that when we see Amon Goeth approaching from a distance, we had to work. Because that happened over and over again. And he had a habit of putting on a white shawl. You know those elegant white shawls that you wear with evening dinner suits to the opera? Like a scarf?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, a scarf, long scarf. Well, when had-- when he had that on, that meant he was going to enjoy himself. He was going to shoot people. And that's really how it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: So later on, I managed to get out of that barrack. My parents arrived in March. In March, the ghetto was liquidated. And my parents really came to the camp. So I felt more secure. I felt-- well, we're all together. And we are in Plaszow, not that far from where we lived, where-- the area that I knew. The war will finish, and we'll go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was far from the truth. [SIGHS] My mother was assigned to peel potatoes for the camp, for the army, for the SS, for everybody. The elderly women-- elderly, my mother was in her 40s at that stage. And whoever had a little bit of protection, even if they were younger, they were sitting in that peeling room-- [NON-ENGLISH] it was called-- and had opportunity to eat raw potatoes or put in a pocket potato and bring it to us. And there were little iron ovens. We could cook the potatoes. And that supplemented our meager rations. But in Plaszow, we still get sort of a coffee in the morning and piece of bread, which we were told it consists of sawdust, to make it go further. Didn't taste very well. But oh boy, we were eating. And we have learn in time that it's not very good to go and stand for-- in queue for the soup first, but to go in the middle. Because in the middle, you got few potatoes and few potato shells, and whatever else that was floating in that, in that container with the soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in the evening-- a portion of bread and a bit of soup. And that was the whole ration. So when Mother brought a potato from [NON-ENGLISH], it was really something.

speaker: interviewee
text: I also must tell you that at the beginning, in Plaszow, my mother was very lucky to be chosen to go out of the Plaszow camp, to work. And while she was going to work, occasionally she would meet somebody, a man whom she knew before the war. He used to sweep the streets in front of my father's office. And father would give him pay for it and give him extra.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was very nice to my mother. And mother arranged with him that he should try to cook something and bring her food. And he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mother had big black coat. See, in Plaszow, we still retained our own clothes. And in the lining of her coat, she would put the pan, the, the, saucepan with soup, with potatoes, with a whole chicken.

speaker: interviewee
text: That Stanislaw was kind enough to hand it to Mother on the bridge. You know on a bridge they have those little, big, whatever-- what are they called? The bridge is hold, held by-- Pylons?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you. And he would sweep. While he was sweeping, he would lift it up, pass it on to my mother. And the guard, which was taking people to work, they were not as severe and not as sadistic as the SS which guarded the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: So very often Mother would bring something to eat. And we would eat it on top of the bunks. And the rest in the block would say, something smells, who's got something? Come on, tell us. They all wanted a little bit. So we had to share with the immediate people on the bunks. And we did that gladly-- not gladly. But Mother always did share. We always had close people to ask, whom we knew from before the war, from ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a girlfriend Paula [NON-ENGLISH], which I went to school with. She slept with me on top. And my mother was on the lower, lowest bunk, so she wouldn't have to climb. And we would share. And they always patiently waited till I had my share. Mother was very concerned about my growing so fast and not having enough food. Well, that was a best recipe for TB. Even before the war, they were worried about me. And you know, I went through the camp, and I didn't get TB. I got lots of other things. I had typhus, but not TB, thanks God.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your daily life like in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we used to go to work. There were working places. It was called gemeinschaft. And gemeinschaft, that means communal working place. And there were benches. And there were chairs. And I used to work in a place plaiting straw, from which we were making big shoes for the army, for German army, who went into East. And they were putting that on top of their boots, so that they wouldn't freeze. And later, there was also paper gemeinschaft, the communal working place. And we were making paper bags. This was all source for the Germans to make money. They were selling this or giving to-- I don't know, probably sending to Germany. That's, that's what it must have been.

speaker: interviewer
text: I often wondered, what do they need paper bags for? I mean, there was no food in Poland. But it didn't occur to me that they were sending home to Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was also a [NON-ENGLISH], a leather, leather factory. And we were sewing on a little horse, on a proper horse with two needles. We were sawing the cover for a gun, belts, and mainly the, the gun holders. And I have learned how to sew with those two needles and that little pony. And the pony was a marvelous source of sleeping. Because when that fellow who was watching us was at the other end of the bench, well, I was managing-- I managed to put my head down and sleep. And now I realize that that was also a form of depression. You know, one needed that bit of a break. [SIGHS] And we also have learned to make little photo montage and use the leather to make a little, sort of like a little something to hold onto it, to preserve the little pictures that we brought with us. In Plaszow, we still retained our clothes. Although we went to wash. But we received our own clothes after. The lice started a little later. When the summer came, then it was unbearable. But so we did go to have a wash, I think once a week, to have a shower, and quick, wet, out of that shower into the clothes. No towels. No soap. Or if there was, it was only on a running pace. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You know, there are these scenes that come to me, when I think of Plaszow. And I also very often used to hide. Because I was too tired, not enough food. Waiting for my mother to come home, to see what she brought from that work place, [NON-ENGLISH]. That's where she worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] . It was a place where they were collecting produce. But not that Mother had access to it. They only used to go to clean, to clean the place. Occasionally she would meet somebody in the street, and they would give her a bit of food. But that didn't last terribly long. They have cut off the going out to work constantly. The working places were taken by the Poles. They had to go to work.

speaker: interviewee
text: So Mother became redundant. And she looked for other places. And she ended up again in that potato-peeling place. Because that was also-- the purpose was to get some potatoes, to get something to eat, to bring some to my father and to me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Mother made sure that she eats down there. Because they were allowed, really, to cook a little bit of potatoes at lunchtime and eat. But they were not allowed to eat raw potatoes. Because they would eat the stock up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, your whole family was together in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my mother, and myself. My sister was with us in ghetto. I did not tell you that my sister-- when we have, when the parents have heard that ghetto is being liquidated, she has found somebody who wanted to take her as wife, over the border, to help her to escape to another country. And she did. She went via Bochnia, it's a place in Galicia. And down there they have met somebody. And naturally they bribed him well. And the whole family of my brother-in-law managed to escape through the forest, into Czechoslovakia. And from Czechoslovakia, they were sent to Budapest. Or they wanted to go to Budapest. Budapest was at that stage still fairly easy to survive. The Jews were not sent to camps. And they went there as Poles. They managed to get papers, false papers, that they are Poles. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: So that's why your sister wasn't with you in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my brother was sent right at the beginning, before we went even to ghetto, he was sent to Lwow. My mother had sister living in Lwow, her oldest sister, who had no children. And she was in care of orphanage. And her husband was army officer, Polish army officer. So that was a good place for my brother to survive. My parents-- I remember like today-- gave him him a rucksack full of clothes and food, a beautiful long coat. He was also very tall, very good-looking boy. I have grandson, which he reminds me so much of my brother, Avi. And he went to Lwow. Now in Lwow, everything was lovely. My auntie looked after him. We never got in touch with him again. We only know via somebody else that when the Russians came to Lwow, they ask all the people who were there-- Jews and non-Jews-- if they want to opt for Germany or Russia. So my brother in his naivete felt, well, we were not killed. We're sitting comfortably in ghetto. Little did they know. And he has signed petition, yes, he'd like to go back to Krakow, to be with his family. He missed the family. He missed my parents. And that was sufficient for the Russians to send him on a transport to Siberia. And after the war, I have met somebody in Krakow who told me, your brother died in Siberia, of typhus. They were also ill-treated. And the fact that he didn't want to go to Poland, to the, to-- that he wanted to go to Poland, not to go to the Russians, they felt that he is betraying the cause.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- So that's how there was just--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's where my brother went. We never saw him again. He was 17 years old. He was a little older than I was. I remember the day so well when he left. With such an enthusiasm he left. It was such an adventure for him to go. To my parents, it was, again, you know, they wept. But they felt they're doing the right thing by him. And a lot of people went to Russia and survived the war, certainly better to some extent than we did in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was just you and your father and mother in Plaszow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay--

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- that's correct, in Plaszow, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were the three of you together, all through Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. First of all, I stay in the barrack with my mother, which was enormously satisfying. I had my mother to myself. I was the youngest. And believe it or not, before the war I was still going to sleep with my mother's arm. My mother was short, cuddly, nice big bust, warm. And I always cried, I want to go to sleep with my mother. And Mother would oblige and come to bed with me. And I was totally disappointed that in the morning I wake up without my mother's arm. But-- and that's, I felt I still had my mother. It was lovely. And my father was on a different-- in a different block. Because men and women were separated by wire, barbed wire. But nevertheless, we manage to get in touch. We manage to get together, being in the latrine, or being into go into the block. It was dangerous but not that dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- well, every moment was dangerous. The whole process of living in a camp was one danger. So what's a little bit more?

speaker: interviewee
text: But one day, suddenly, I have learned-- I was working in the gemeinschaft. And I have come back from work in the evening, it was dark, and found out that my mother was taken away on a transport. Well, I said, this is it. They took all the old ladies-- 40, 42-- and the young girls who managed to get into that place to work. They were taken on transport. And I was sure Mother was taken. Not even thinking about anything else, I ran down to where the trains were standing with the people loaded. The little windows-- it was a cattle trucks. There were little top windows, barbed wire. And I-- my thoughts were to go and be with my mother together. Wherever she goes, I like to go. And on the way there, snow was on the, on the ground. And Jan, SS man Jan-- short tubby fellow-- caught me and started to whip me with [NON-ENGLISH], with a whip. Wouldn't let me get to the trains close. Shooed me away. By that time, the trains were already locked, ready to go. They had the contingent. He has beaten me very much. So I was totally destroyed. I said, well, they're taking them to Auschwitz. They didn't take me because I was still looking young enough to get some work out of me. And I went to my father's block. My father was equally destroyed. Both of us thought my mother went to Auschwitz. From then on, my father went very depressed, didn't want to go to work, didn't want to eat. The bread which he received, changed for cigarettes. And that was very tragic for me, to watch that. It sort of continued for a while. When he did go to work, he used to fall over, because he has not eaten. He was beaten, he was kicked. He developed ulcers on his leg, varicose veins, ulcers, which in best of times is difficult to heal.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he just couldn't go to work anymore. We're just going to stop and change the tape.
